primary_diagnosis.name: Cutaneous herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection of the right infraorbital cheek in high-risk periocular location — grouped vesicles on an erythematous base with a neuropathic prodrome, no current ocular involvement but a real risk of herpes simplex keratitis that cannot be examined for over telehealth

differential.evidence_basis: A tight cluster of vesicles on an erythematous base with a 48-hour tingling/burning prodrome, in a patient with a past history of cold sores and recent stress + UV triggers — the classic picture of HSV reactivation; the periocular site raises the stakes because of the keratitis risk.

Any vesicular eruption near the eye mandates considering V1 zoster, which carries a high ocular-complication risk (Hutchinson sign at the nasal tip predicts it). Screened against here by the V2 cheek distribution, the absence of forehead/scalp/tip-of-nose lesions and the mild non-neuralgic pain, but not fully excludable remotely.

The early golden/honey crusting can mimic impetigo, but the neuropathic prodrome and intact tense grouped vesicles preceding the crust point to primary HSV; secondary bacterial infection of the HSV lesions remains possible.
Can cause a vesicular facial eruption, but is typically itchy, less sharply grouped and lacks the discrete unilateral cluster on an erythematous base with a neuropathic prodrome — arguing against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is periocular cutaneous HSV; the important-not-to-miss consideration is herpes zoster ophthalmicus, and the dominant disposition driver is the un-examinable ocular (keratitis) risk conferred by the location — not diagnostic doubt about HSV itself.

differential.diagnosis: Cutaneous herpes simplex virus infection (periocular), no current ocular involvement
differential.evidence_basis: No current eye pain/redness/photophobia/visual change by patient report, mild non-neuralgic pain, V2-only distribution with no Hutchinson-sign or forehead lesions, systemically well and afebrile — cutaneous HSV without (yet) demonstrable keratitis, but the cornea cannot be examined remotely.

Screened negative by patient report (eye clear, vision intact), but an early dendritic keratitis can be present with minimal symptoms and needs slit-lamp + fluorescein to exclude — the exact assessment telehealth cannot do, which is why urgent in-person eye review is the correct disposition.

Argued against by distribution and pain character but retained as a safety-net, since if it were zoster the ocular-complication risk would be higher still and would similarly mandate urgent ophthalmology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes active ocular involvement, orbital cellulitis and V1 zoster on current evidence, but the sight-threatening keratitis risk is un-examinable over telehealth — so the correct endpoint is urgent same-day in-person eye assessment plus antiviral, NOT routine cold-sore self-care and NOT a 000 ambulance.

differential.diagnosis: Periocular cutaneous HSV infection with an un-examinable keratitis risk, immunocompetent adult
differential.evidence_basis: Classic grouped vesicles with prodrome and HSV triggers, eye clear on patient report but cornea not examinable remotely; the correct end-point is recognition of periocular HSV as a sight-threat requiring urgent same-day in-person eye assessment, commencement of high-dose episodic oral antiviral, strict autoinoculation-prevention advice, and explicit ocular safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognising cutaneous HSV in a periocular location as materially different from a routine lip cold sore: it needs urgent same-day in-person examination of the eye (which telehealth cannot do), antiviral therapy and hand-hygiene advice. Under-triage (routine cold-sore self-care, missed ocular risk) and over-triage (000 ambulance for a currently-clear eye) are both scored errors.

red_flags.description: Herpes simplex keratitis / ocular HSV involvement — eye pain, redness, photophobia, gritty/foreign-body sensation, watering, discharge or any blurring or reduction of vision; a corneal dendritic ulcer on fluorescein staining.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The sight-threatening complication of periocular HSV; untreated or steroid-exposed herpetic keratitis can cause corneal scarring and permanent visual loss. The cornea cannot be examined (visual acuity, slit-lamp, fluorescein) over telehealth, so this is the red flag that drives the urgent in-person disposition.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed or evolving herpes simplex keratitis progresses to corneal ulceration and scarring, with lasting or permanent vision loss.

red_flags.description: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1) features — deep severe neuralgic pain, vesicles on the forehead, scalp, upper eyelid, or the tip/side of the nose (Hutchinson sign).
red_flags.clinical_significance: V1 zoster carries an even higher ocular-complication rate than HSV; the Hutchinson sign (nasociliary involvement) predicts ocular disease. Distinguishing it changes urgency and antiviral dosing and mandates ophthalmology if ocular involvement is suspected.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised herpes zoster ophthalmicus can cause keratitis, uveitis and vision loss and warrants prompt high-dose antiviral and ophthalmology review.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial spread / periorbital or orbital cellulitis — spreading facial or eyelid erythema and swelling, fever, pain or restriction on eye movement, or proptosis.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Periocular skin infection can progress to preseptal/orbital cellulitis, a vision- and life-threatening emergency; the early golden crusting also flags possible bacterial superinfection of the HSV lesions.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated orbital cellulitis can cause vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis and intracranial spread.

pathophysiology_summary: Herpes simplex virus establishes lifelong latency in sensory (trigeminal) ganglia after primary infection and periodically reactivates, travelling down the sensory nerve to produce a localised mucocutaneous eruption. Reactivation is precipitated by triggers such as psychological stress, ultraviolet exposure, fever and local trauma — here a recent period of intense stress and beach/UV exposure. The lesion evolves through a neuropathic prodrome (tingling/burning in the affected dermatomal skin) to a tight cluster of tense vesicles on an erythematous base, which then unroof to shallow ulcers and crust, healing over 7-10 days. The clinical significance in THIS case is entirely the location: an eruption on the infraorbital cheek sits within the ophthalmic risk zone, and HSV can extend to or autoinoculate the ocular surface, producing herpes simplex keratitis — the commonest infectious cause of corneal blindness — which classically forms a branching dendritic corneal ulcer visible only on fluorescein staining and slit-lamp examination. The key differential, herpes zoster ophthalmicus, reactivates the V1 (ophthalmic) division of the trigeminal nerve and carries an even higher ocular-complication risk, signposted by the Hutchinson sign (vesicles on the nasal tip). Because the cornea, visual acuity and early dendritic ulceration cannot be assessed over telehealth, the whole clinical task is to recognise periocular HSV as a sight-threat that mandates urgent in-person eye examination and prompt antiviral therapy, rather than treating it as an ordinary cold sore — while critically avoiding topical corticosteroids, which can convert a herpetic keratitis into a sight-destroying geographic ulcer.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recognising HSV but treating it like an ordinary lip cold sore — advising topical antiviral cream and self-care with no eye assessment — thereby missing that a PERIOCULAR eruption carries an un-examinable keratitis risk that mandates urgent in-person eye review and systemic antiviral.

Reaching for a topical corticosteroid to calm the 'inflamed' rash near the eye — corticosteroids applied to (or reaching) an active herpetic eye lesion can precipitate or dramatically worsen dendritic/geographic keratitis and cause corneal perforation. A classic, dangerous error of commission in periocular HSV.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the ocular and zoster screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling the rash as HSV but never screening for eye pain/redness/photophobia/visual change (keratitis) or for forehead/tip-of-nose lesions and deep neuralgic pain (herpes zoster ophthalmicus) — omitting the very screen that sets the urgency and the disposition.

Being swept by the patient's fear about their sight into calling a 000 ambulance for a systemically-well patient with a currently-clear eye, when the proportionate action is urgent SAME-DAY in-person eye assessment (GP/ophthalmology), not an emergency ambulance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attempting to reassure the eye is safe from history alone
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Concluding the eye is fine because the patient reports normal vision and no redness — an early dendritic keratitis can be near-asymptomatic, and only slit-lamp/fluorescein examination excludes it, so a negative patient-reported eye screen justifies urgent in-person assessment, not remote reassurance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Ophthalmic examination — visual acuity testing, conjunctival/corneal inspection, slit-lamp and fluorescein staining for a dendritic keratitis, and extraocular-movement assessment (source in-person exam found visual acuity grossly intact, no conjunctival injection, no corneal clouding, no dendritic ulcers on gross inspection and painless normal eye movements)
Palpation of regional lymph nodes (source in-person exam found tender right submandibular lymphadenopathy)
Direct inspection to confirm the grouped-vesicle-on-erythematous-base morphology and the exact distance of the lesion from the eyelid margin (source ~1.5 cm from the lower lid)
Exact vital signs without a home device (source in-person values BP 132/84, HR 88, Temp 37.3, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Wt 74 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise cutaneous herpes simplex in a high-risk periocular location as a potential sight-threat that must NOT be managed as a routine cold sore; actively screen for ocular involvement (eye pain/redness/photophobia/gritty sensation/visual change), herpes zoster ophthalmicus (forehead/tip-of-nose lesions, deep neuralgic pain) and cellulitis (spreading swelling, fever); arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP with capacity to examine the eye, or ophthalmology) to perform visual acuity and fluorescein/slit-lamp examination and to commence high-dose episodic oral antiviral therapy (the source used valaciclovir); give strict hand-hygiene / do-not-touch-the-eye autoinoculation advice and simple analgesia. Escalate to same-day emergency/ophthalmology (T4) if any ocular red flag, cellulitis feature or zoster feature appears; a 000 ambulance is not warranted for a currently-clear eye.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the skin diagnosis (grouped vesicles + prodrome + HSV triggers are classic). The dominant risks are UNDER-action (treating periocular HSV as a routine cold sore and missing the un-examinable keratitis risk) and, in the other direction, distress-driven OVER-escalation to a 000 ambulance when urgent same-day in-person eye assessment is the proportionate response.
